natural_occurrence.description: Aluminium is the most abundant metal in Earth's crust but never occurs naturally in pure metallic form. Food-grade metallic aluminium is produced industrially from bauxite ore.

manufacturing.description: Produced through the Hall-Héroult process: extracting aluminium from bauxite ore through electrolysis. The metal is then ground into fine powder or flakes for use as a surface colorant in food decoration.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used in eye shadows, nail polish, and other cosmetics for metallic/shimmering effects.
other_uses.industrial: Widely used in construction, transportation, packaging (foil), electrical applications, and aerospace.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used as a coating for pills and tablets for visual appeal.
other_uses.household: Aluminium foil, cooking utensils, beverage cans.

eu.conditions: Only for external decoration of confectionery and cake decorations. Not permitted for internal use.

usa.notes: Not approved for use as a food colorant in the United States

japan.notes: Restricted to external surface decoration only

australia_nz.notes: Not permitted for use in food

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (limited oral absorption; use restricted to surface decoration)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (minimal exposure due to external use only)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA reviews aluminium and confirms safety for external surface decoration only
USA and Australia do not approve E173 for food use despite EU approval

food_categories_eu.category_name: Decorations, coatings and fillings (external coating only)
